quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier represents a strictly larger percentage decline in global obesity rates, moving from a negligible 1% reduction to a transformative 50% reduction. The core scenario—a worldwide decline in obesity—remains constant across all tiers, with only the magnitude of the decline varying. The spacing between tiers widens at higher levels to reflect the increasing difficulty and impact of larger reductions.

preference_direction: A greater decline in global obesity rates represents improved public health outcomes, reduced disease burden, lower healthcare costs, and enhanced quality of life for millions of people, making tier 7 (50% decline) the most preferable outcome.
v1_broken_audit_rationale: This does not implement the required neutral-midpoint design: T4 is not a bare baseline, and tiers 1-3 are not negative deviations from it but merely smaller versions of the same positive outcome. Although the ladder is monotonic in obesity reduction, it fails the intended 3-below / baseline / 3-above parametric structure.
v2_fix_explanation: The original T4 ('decline by 10%') was already a positive outcome, making all seven tiers positive deviations rather than spanning negative-to-positive around a neutral baseline. I reset T4 to 'rates remain unchanged' (a true neutral, unqualified baseline) and built three symmetric steps of worsening (increases) below it and three symmetric steps of improvement (declines) above it.
